questions.question_text: An agent and a seller sign a listing agreement. This creates agency by:
questions.explanation: Agency by express agreement is created when the principal and agent explicitly agree to an agency relationship, either in writing or verbally. A signed listing agreement is the clearest example of express agency — it specifically identifies the parties, defines the agent's authority, sets the compensation, and establishes the term. Most states require listing agreements to be in writing. Express agency is the most common and preferred method of creating agency because the parties' rights and obligations are clearly defined.

questions.question_text: A buyer tours properties with an agent and shares their maximum budget, must-haves, and timeline. No buyer representation agreement is signed. An agency relationship may have been created by:
distractors: No agency is possible without a written agreement
questions.explanation: Implied agency arises from the parties' conduct rather than an explicit agreement. When a buyer shares confidential information (budget, motivation, timeline) with an agent who acts in an advisory capacity without clarifying their role, an implied agency relationship may be created. This is why disclosure of agency relationships is critical — the agent should clarify who they represent before the buyer shares anything confidential. Without clarity, the buyer may reasonably believe they are being represented, creating unintended fiduciary obligations for the agent.

questions.question_text: A buyer meets a listing agent at an open house. The buyer shares that they must buy quickly due to a job relocation and could go as high as $350,000, though the home is listed at $310,000. The listing agent's BEST course of action is to:
questions.correct_answer: Clarify their role as the seller's agent before the buyer shares more
distractors: Keep the buyer's information confidential since conversations are privileged
Stop the conversation and refer the buyer to a different agent
Accept the information but treat it as confidential since shared privately
questions.explanation: A listing agent represents the seller. Information the buyer shares about their motivation, timeline, and maximum price is exactly what the seller needs to negotiate effectively — and the listing agent is obligated to share it with the seller. The agent must disclose their role as the seller's representative before the buyer shares confidential information, so the buyer understands that this information will not be protected. Failure to clarify the agency relationship at the outset creates confusion and potential liability. Agency disclosure requirements exist precisely to prevent this scenario.

questions.question_text: An agent submits an offer on behalf of a buyer without authorization. The buyer later reviews the offer and approves it by signing. Agency was created by:
questions.explanation: Ratification creates agency (or expands it) when a principal knowingly approves an agent's unauthorized act after it occurs. For ratification to be effective: the agent must have acted on behalf of the principal (not for themselves), the principal must have full knowledge of the material facts, and the principal must affirm the entire transaction (not just part of it). Once ratified, the unauthorized act is treated as if it had been authorized from the beginning. This is distinct from estoppel, where a third party relied on apparent authority before the principal later tries to deny it.

questions.question_text: A listing agreement expires without the property selling. The agency relationship:
distractors: Continues until the seller formally notifies the broker in writing
Converts to a month-to-month listing the seller must actively cancel
Terminates only after the broker returns all keys and materials
questions.explanation: A listing agreement specifies a term, and agency automatically terminates when that term expires. The seller does not need to take any action — the agency simply ends. Other events that automatically terminate agency include: completion of the transaction (purpose fulfilled), destruction of the subject property, death or legal incapacity of either party, and bankruptcy of the principal. Events that require action include: revocation by the principal (may owe compensation if done without cause before expiration) and renunciation by the agent (may be liable for breach of contract if they abandon the engagement prematurely).

questions.question_text: A seller fires their listing agent two months before the listing agreement expires because they decided not to sell. The agent had been actively marketing the property. The seller:
questions.correct_answer: May revoke but could owe compensation for early termination
distractors: Cannot revoke the listing before the expiration date under any circumstances
May revoke freely at any time with no financial consequences
Must sell to the broker's buyer if one has been found
questions.explanation: A principal always has the power to revoke an agency relationship because personal services cannot be compelled (specific performance is not available for personal service contracts). However, revoking without cause before expiration may breach the listing contract — the broker may be entitled to compensation for work already performed or damages. The listing agreement terms govern what compensation, if any, is owed. Importantly: power to revoke ≠ right to revoke without consequence. The seller has the power but may not have the legal right to revoke without liability.

questions.question_text: Express agency may be created by either a written OR an oral agreement. However, most states require listing agreements to be:
distractors: Notarized and recorded at the county recorder's office
Approved by the state real estate commission before marketing
questions.explanation: While express agency can theoretically be created orally, most states require listing agreements to be in writing under the Statute of Frauds (because they involve the sale of real property or a brokerage service agreement lasting beyond one year in some cases). A written listing agreement protects both the seller and the broker by clearly defining the terms: listing price, commission rate, duration, marketing authority, and the agent's duties. Oral listings, even if made, are generally unenforceable.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a buyer representation agreement with a broker. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: A buyer representation agreement is an express creation of agency — the parties explicitly agree in writing to establish an agent-principal relationship. The agreement defines the scope of representation, the broker's duties, the compensation arrangement, and the term. This is the most definitive way to create a buyer agency relationship. Implied agency arises from conduct. Ostensible (apparent) agency arises from third-party perceptions. Gratuitous agency involves an agent who agrees to work without compensation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a method of creating an agency relationship?
questions.explanation: Agency requires mutual consent — it cannot be created by one party's unilateral declaration. Simply announcing 'I am your agent' does not create agency without the principal's agreement or conduct suggesting acceptance. The recognized methods of creating agency are: express agreement (written or oral contract), implication (conduct of the parties), ratification (principal approves unauthorized acts), and estoppel (third party relies on apparent authority). All require some form of bilateral involvement.

questions.question_text: Agency by estoppel occurs when:
questions.correct_answer: A principal's conduct leads a third party to believe an agency exists
distractors: An agent is physically prevented from performing duties by outside force
A court order establishes an agency relationship against one party's will
A principal and agent mutually agree to stop the agency early
questions.explanation: Agency by estoppel arises when a principal's words or actions cause a third party to reasonably believe that someone is their agent, and the third party relies on that belief to their detriment. The principal is then 'estopped' (legally prevented) from denying the agency. For example, if a property owner allows someone to show their property and negotiate on their behalf without clarifying that the person is not their authorized agent, the owner may be bound by that person's commitments to a buyer who reasonably relied on the apparent authority.

questions.question_text: An agent who has been showing properties to a buyer for weeks without a written agreement may have created:
questions.explanation: Implied agency arises from the conduct of the parties. When an agent consistently acts in an advisory capacity — showing properties, providing market analysis, counseling on offers — the buyer may reasonably believe they are being represented, creating an implied agency. This is a significant risk for agents who fail to clarify their role. The remedy is to always establish the relationship in writing at the outset. Not all states default to transaction brokerage for unwritten relationships.

questions.question_text: To prevent inadvertent implied agency, most states require agents to:
questions.correct_answer: Provide agency disclosure early in the relationship
distractors: Refuse to speak with any party who has not signed a representation agreement
Record all conversations with prospects to prove no agency was created
Obtain a court declaration of non-agency before showing property
questions.explanation: Agency disclosure requirements exist to prevent implied agency situations. Most states require agents to disclose who they represent at the first substantive contact with a prospective buyer or seller. This typically involves presenting a written disclosure form that explains the different types of agency relationships and identifies who the agent represents. Early disclosure protects both the agent (from unintended fiduciary obligations) and the consumer (from mistakenly sharing confidential information with the wrong party's agent).

questions.question_text: A seller tells a neighbor, 'My agent Bob will handle everything if you want to buy my house.' Bob has no listing agreement. The neighbor offers to buy and Bob facilitates the sale. This is agency by:
questions.explanation: Agency by estoppel applies when a principal's actions cause a third party to reasonably believe an agency exists. The seller told the neighbor that Bob was their agent, and the neighbor relied on that representation. The seller is now estopped from denying the agency. This differs from ratification (where the principal approves after the unauthorized act) and express agreement (where both principal and agent explicitly agree). The seller's statement to the neighbor, combined with the neighbor's reliance, creates estoppel.

questions.question_text: For ratification to be effective, the principal must:
questions.correct_answer: Have full knowledge of the material facts before approving
distractors: File a written ratification with the state real estate commission
Approve the act within 24 hours of learning about it
Obtain the other party's consent before ratifying the action
questions.explanation: Ratification requires: (1) the agent acted on behalf of the principal (not for themselves), (2) the principal has full knowledge of the material facts of the transaction, and (3) the principal affirms the entire act. No filing, specific timeframe, or other-party consent is required. The principal cannot selectively ratify only favorable parts of the transaction — it is all or nothing. Once ratified, the act relates back to the original date and is treated as if authorized from the start.

questions.question_text: A real estate agent negotiates a deal without the seller's authorization. The seller learns of the deal and signs the contract. The seller has:
distractors: Created an express agency by signing
Waived all claims against the agent
questions.explanation: By signing the contract with full knowledge, the seller has ratified the agent's unauthorized negotiation. The transaction is now binding as if the agent had been authorized all along. This does not automatically create a new listing agreement — ratification confirms the specific act, not an ongoing relationship. The transaction is valid, not fraudulent. Ratification does not necessarily waive the seller's right to pursue disciplinary complaints — the agent still acted without authorization initially, which may be a license violation even if the outcome was ratified.

questions.question_text: A property manager collects rent on behalf of an owner without a formal property management agreement. The owner accepts the rent checks each month. This is agency by:
questions.explanation: The owner's repeated acceptance of rent collected by the manager constitutes ratification of the manager's authority to collect on the owner's behalf. Each acceptance is an approval of the unauthorized act. While this could also involve elements of implied agency (the owner's conduct suggests acceptance), the clearest classification is ratification because the owner has actual knowledge and accepts the specific benefits. Express agency would require a written or explicit verbal agreement, not just passive acceptance.

questions.question_text: A principal can ratify only the favorable parts of an unauthorized transaction and reject the rest. This statement is:
questions.explanation: Ratification is an all-or-nothing proposition. The principal must accept the entire unauthorized act — they cannot cherry-pick favorable terms while rejecting unfavorable ones. If an agent negotiated a $300,000 sale with a 60-day closing and the seller only wanted a 30-day closing, the seller must either ratify the entire deal (including the 60-day close) or reject it entirely. This prevents principals from unfairly taking only the benefits of unauthorized acts while avoiding the burdens.

questions.question_text: Which of the following events will automatically terminate an agency relationship?
questions.correct_answer: Death of either the principal or the agent
distractors: The agent taking a vacation for two weeks during the listing period
A disagreement between the agent and principal over marketing strategy
The principal receiving an offer that is below the listing price
questions.explanation: Agency is a personal relationship that automatically terminates upon the death of either party. Other events that automatically terminate agency include: incapacity of either party, destruction of the subject property, bankruptcy of the principal, and expiration of the agency term. A vacation, a disagreement, or a low offer do not terminate agency — they are normal occurrences during the listing period. The agent's duty to perform continues during the listing term regardless of these situations.

questions.question_text: An agent abandons a listing by ceasing all marketing efforts and not returning the seller's calls. This is called:
questions.explanation: Renunciation is the agent's unilateral abandonment of the agency relationship. While an agent has the power to renounce (walk away), doing so without cause before the listing term expires may constitute a breach of the listing agreement, exposing the agent to liability for damages. Revocation is the principal's (seller's) termination of the agency. Expiration is automatic at the end of the agreed term. Rescission involves canceling due to a formation defect like fraud or mutual mistake.

questions.question_text: A protection period (or safety clause) in a listing agreement means that:
questions.correct_answer: The broker earns a commission if a listed buyer closes after expiration
distractors: The seller is shielded from liability during the listing term
The broker receives a minimum commission regardless of sale
Neither party may cancel the listing for 60 days
questions.explanation: A protection period (also called a carryover or safety clause) protects the broker's commission after the listing expires. If a buyer who was introduced or shown the property during the listing period purchases the property within the protection period (typically 30-180 days after expiration), the original broker is entitled to the commission. The broker must typically provide the seller with a list of prospective buyers before the listing expires. This prevents sellers from waiting for the listing to expire to avoid paying commission on a deal the broker facilitated.
